perturbation.original_text: 1.16 "SpinCo Intellectual Property" shall mean (a) the Registrable IP set forth on Schedule 1.16 and (b) all Other IP owned by, licensed by or to, or sublicensed by or to either Party or any member of its Group as of the Effective Time that is dedicated to the SpinCo Business, including any Other IP set forth on Schedule 1.16; provided, however, that SpinCo Intellectual Property does not include any Registrable IP or Other IP that comprises (i) Mill Recovery Technology/Intellectual Property, or (ii) Pre-applied Adhesives Technology/Intellectual Property.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.16 "SpinCo Intellectual Property" shall mean (a) the Registrable IP set forth on Schedule 1.16 and (b) all Other IP owned by, licensed by or to, or sublicensed by or to either Party or any member of its Group as of the Effective Time that is dedicated to the SpinCo Business, including any Other IP set forth on Schedule 1.16; provided, however, that SpinCo Intellectual Property includes any Registrable IP or Other IP that comprises (i) Mill Recovery Technology/Intellectual Property, or (ii) Pre-applied Adhesives Technology/Intellectual Property.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "SpinCo Intellectual Property" in Section 1.16 is altered to include “Mill Recovery Technology/Intellectual Property” and “Pre-applied Adhesives Technology/Intellectual Property”, which it explicitly excludes in the original definition. This introduces an in-text contradiction. The original definition specifically excludes those items, meaning they are not considered part of SpinCo's Intellectual Property. The contradicted_location is where they are considered to be explicitly excluded, creating the contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.16 "SpinCo Intellectual Property" shall mean (a) the Registrable IP set forth on Schedule 1.16 and (b) all Other IP owned by, licensed by or to, or sublicensed by or to either Party or any member of its Group as of the Effective Time that is dedicated to the SpinCo Business, including any Other IP set forth on Schedule 1.16; provided, however, that SpinCo Intellectual Property includes any Registrable IP or Other IP that comprises (i) Mill Recovery Technology/Intellectual Property, or (ii) Pre-applied Adhesives Technology/Intellectual Property.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'SpinCo Intellectual Property' to *include* 'Mill Recovery Technology/Intellectual Property' and 'Pre-applied Adhesives Technology/Intellectual Property', while the contradicted text, located at the same section in the contract, originally *excludes* those items. This direct reversal of inclusion/exclusion creates a clear and direct conflict and in-text contradiction. This creates ambiguity and contradicts the text regarding which items should be included as part of 'SpinCo Intellectual Property,' fitting the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 License Grant to Parent. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, SpinCo hereby grants to each individual member of the Parent Group, on behalf of itself and the other members of the SpinCo Group, and shall cause the other members of the SpinCo Group to grant to each individual member of the Parent Group, a non-exclusive, worldwide, perpetual, irrevocable, fully paid-up, royalty-free right and license, for use in the Parent Field, to (i) use, reproduce, distribute, display, perform, make Improvements and exploit the Licensed SpinCo IP, and (ii) make, have made, use, sell, offer to sell and import any goods and services incorporating, embodying or utilizing the Licensed SpinCo IP.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 License Grant to Parent. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, SpinCo hereby grants to each individual member of the Parent Group, on behalf of itself and the other members of the SpinCo Group, and shall cause the other members of the SpinCo Group to grant to each individual member of the Parent Group, a non-exclusive, worldwide, temporary, revocable, fully paid-up, royalty-free right and license, for use in the Parent Field, to (i) use, reproduce, distribute, display, perform, make Improvements and exploit the Licensed SpinCo IP, and (ii) make, have made, use, sell, offer to sell and import any goods and services incorporating, embodying or utilizing the Licensed SpinCo IP.
perturbation.explanation: The terms of the license granted to Parent in Section 3.1 are changed from 'perpetual, irrevocable' to 'temporary, revocable.' This introduces an in-text contradiction with the later statement about 'Bankruptcy' in Section 3.7, which relies on the license being treated as intellectual property under bankruptcy law and thus implicitly assumes the license is perpetual.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.7 Bankruptcy. In the event that this Agreement is terminated or rejected by SpinCo, a member of the SpinCo Group or its receiver or trustee under applicable bankruptcy laws due to such Party's bankruptcy, then all rights and licenses granted under or pursuant to this Agreement by SpinCo to Parent are, and shall otherwise be deemed to be, for purposes of Section 365(n) of the U.S. Bankruptcy Code (the "Code") and any similar laws in any other country, licenses of rights to "intellectual property" as defined under the Code for purposes of Section 365(n). The Parties agree that all intellectual property rights licensed hereunder, including, without limitation, any patents or patent applications in any country of SpinCo or a member of SpinCo Group covered by the license grants under this Agreement, are part of the "intellectual property" as defined under the Code for purposes of Section 365(n) subject to the protections afforded the non-terminating Party under Section 365(n) of the Code, and any similar law or regulation in any other country.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'perpetual, irrevocable' to 'temporary, revocable' in the license granted to Parent directly contradicts the assumption in Section 3.7 that the license is treated as intellectual property under bankruptcy law, which implicitly relies on a perpetual license. Section 3.7 uses section 365(n) of the U.S. Bankruptcy Code which only applies to licenses of 'intellectual property' which is typically assumed to be a perpetual and irrevocable license not a temporary and revocable license.

perturbation.original_text: 1.5 "Intellectual Property" shall mean all of the following whether arising under the Laws of the United States or of any other foreign or multinational jurisdiction: (a) patents, patent applications (including patents issued thereon) and statutory invention registrations, including reissues, divisions, continuations, continuations in part, substitutions, renewals, extensions and reexaminations of any of the foregoing, and all rights in any of the foregoing provided by international treaties or conventions, (b) trademarks, service marks, trade names, service names, trade dress, logos and other source or business identifiers, including all goodwill associated with any of the foregoing, and any and all common law rights in and to any of the foregoing, registrations and applications for registration of any of the foregoing, all rights in and to any of the foregoing provided by international treaties or conventions, and all reissues, extensions and renewals of any of the foregoing, (c) Internet domain names, accounts with Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter and similar social media platforms, registrations and related rights, (d) copyrightable works, copyrights, moral rights, mask work rights, database rights and design rights, in each case, other than Software, whether or not registered, and all registrations and applications for registration of any of the foregoing, and all rights in and to any of the foregoing provided by international treaties or conventions, (e) confidential and proprietary information, including trade secrets, invention disclosures, processes and know-how and (f) any other intellectual property rights, in each case, other than Software. The items listed in subsections (b) and (c) of this Section 1.5 are referred to herein as "Trademark-Related IP".
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 "Intellectual Property" shall mean only patents and trademarks whether arising under the Laws of the United States or of any other foreign or multinational jurisdiction: (a) patents, patent applications (including patents issued thereon) and statutory invention registrations, including reissues, divisions, continuations, continuations in part, substitutions, renewals, extensions and reexaminations of any of the foregoing, and all rights in any of the foregoing provided by international treaties or conventions, (b) trademarks, service marks, trade names, service names, trade dress, logos and other source or business identifiers, including all goodwill associated with any of the foregoing, and any and all common law rights in and to any of the foregoing, registrations and applications for registration of any of the foregoing, all rights in and to any of the foregoing provided by international treaties or conventions, and all reissues, extensions and renewals of any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Intellectual Property" in Section 1.5 is changed to include only patents and trademarks, thus removing Copyrights, Trade Secrets and Internet domains. This introduces an in-text contradiction with the later statement about "Trade Secret" in Section 3.3 and extends to Section 5.4, which refers to Section 6.9 (Confidentiality) of the Separation Agreement. Since Trade Secrets are no longer part of the Intellectual Property, Section 3.3 creates a conflict of obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3 Neither Party shall make a trade secret of the other Party public or otherwise destroy or impair the trade secret status of such trade secret without the express, advance, written consent of the other Party. Any agreement by which a trade secret is transferred or sublicensed shall be subject to the same confidentiality requirements as stated herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 1.5 redefines "Intellectual Property" to include only patents and trademarks, which conflicts with Section 3.3. Section 3.3 refers to "trade secret," which is no longer included in the definition of "Intellectual Property" after the modification to Section 1.5. This creates a contradiction because Section 3.3 imposes obligations regarding trade secrets, but the altered definition of Intellectual Property no longer encompasses trade secrets, leading to uncertainty about whether those obligations still apply and therefore making the terms incompatible.
